content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Earl D. Nelson 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-121 
Dear Mr. Nelson: 
August 15, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-government employment 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that our advice does not address other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest, Section 1090, or Public Contract Code Section 10411. 
Additionally, we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 
71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
Lastly, this letter should not be construed as evaluating past conduct. The Commission will 
decline to provide advice related to conduct that has already occurred. (Regulation 18329.) 
QUESTION 
Are you prohibited from participating, on behalf of your private employer, on task orders 
related to a contract that you assisted in implementing while serving as a Program Manager for the 
California Department of Water Resources? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. As a former state employee subject to the Act's permanent ban on "switching sides," 
your prior participation in proceedings related to the implementation of that contract precludes you 
from further participating in such proceedings on your employer's behalf. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
The California Department of Water Resources ("DWR") employed you as a Program 
Manager III in its Hydropower License Planning and Compliance Office until you retired on July 
19, 201 7. In November 201 7, you were hired as a Senior Environmental Scientist for Stantec 
Consulting Services. You currently serve in this position. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-18-121 
Page No. 2 
During your tenure with DWR, you served on the contract proposal review team that 
selected MWH Global, Inc. to assist with various steps required for the relicensing of State Water 
Project hydropower facilities at Silverwood Lake, Pyramid Lake, and Castaic Lake (the "Project"). 
You indicate that the contract between DWR and MWH (the "Contract") is limited to time and 
materials and does not authorize any work. 
Work related to the Project was and continues to be considered, authorized, and 
implemented by DWR via individual task orders. Each order has its own scope of work, schedule, · 
detailed costs and deliverables. You managed several of the early task orders arising under the 
Contract. But the orders you managed have all since been completed. 
In 2016, MWH was purchased by Stantec Consulting Services, Inc. Stantec assumed the 
Contract and outstanding task orders. While you do not currently engage in any activities related to 
the Project on behalf of Stantec, you believe they could benefit from your expertise in executing 
related task orders. You request advice as to whether the Act's permanent ban restricts you from 
participating on orders. 
ANALYSIS 
Public officials who have left state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act: the one-year ban and the permanent ban. (Sections 87400-
87406 and Regulation 18746.1.) Our analysis is limited to the permanent ban as you have indicated 
that your last day of employment with DWR was more than one year ago. 
Permanent Ban 
The permanent ban prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and 
participating for compensation in certain proceedings involving the State of California and other 
specific parties, if the proceeding is one in which the former state employee participated while 
employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 18741.1.) It applies to any formal or 
informal appearance or any oral or written communication made with the intent to influence a 
judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding in which you participated while serving as a state 
administrative official. (Section 87401.)2 
An official or employee has "participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the 
proceeding "personally, and substantially" through "decision, approval, disapproval, formal written 
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential 
information .... " (Section 87400(d).) 
The permanent ban does not apply to "new" proceedings even in cases where the new 
proceeding is related to, or grows out of, a prior proceeding in which the official had participated. 
For example, we have previously advised that the implementation of a contract is considered a 
"new" proceeding in relation to the awarding of that contract. (Yam Advice Letter, No. 1-17-292; 
Starovoytov Advice Letter, No. A-14-149). However, where an employee has already participated 
2 The Act defines "Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" as "any proceeding, application, request for a 
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other particular 
matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative agency .... " (Section 87400(c).) 


File No. A-18-121 
Page No. 3 
in the implementation of a contract, he or she may not "switch sides" and work for compensation 
for an outside employer on the same implementation proceeding. (Yam Advice Letter, No. 1-17-
292; Culp Advice Letter, No. 1-14-051.) 
As a former designated employee of DWR, you are subject to the Act's permanent ban on 
"switching sides." During your tenure with DWR, you served as Task Order Manager for several 
task orders implementing the Contract. While those 9rders were distinct from those at issue here -
in that they arose prior to your retirement from state employment - we find that they are all part of 
one general implementation proceeding. In other words, participating in one stage of the 
implementation of a contract as a state employee precludes you from participating, on behalf of a 
private employer, on any future stages of implementation arising from the same contract. 
Accordingly, you may not "participate" on behalf of your employer on future task orders related to 
the Contract by virtue of your past participation. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
AES:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Acting General Counsel 
Adam E. Silver 
By: 
Counsel, Legal Division
